front: Search incident to arrest (SITA): what two areas does a lawful custodial arrest authorize officers to search without a warrant?
back: (1) The arrestee's person, and (2) the area within the arrestee's immediate control (the "wingspan").

front: What are the two rationales (the "twin engines") that justify a search incident to arrest?
back: Officer safety and evidence preservation.

front: SITA: what two predicate conditions must the arrest and search satisfy for the exception to apply?
back: The arrest must be a lawful custodial arrest (not a mere citation), and the search must be contemporaneous with it.

front: Chimel v. California (1969): what is the scope of a search incident to arrest?
back: The arrestee's person plus the area within his immediate control (the "wingspan") — the area from which he might gain a weapon or destructible evidence.

front: Chimel defined "the area within his immediate control" to mean the area from within which he might gain possession of a {{weapon or destructible evidence}}.
back: weapon or destructible evidence

front: Which SCOTUS case is the foundational source of the "wingspan" limit on a search incident to arrest?
back: Chimel v. California (1969).

front: United States v. Robinson (1973): what does a lawful custodial arrest permit as to the arrestee's person?
back: A full search of the person — categorically, with no separate case-by-case showing that weapons or evidence are present.

front: Robinson: "It is the fact of the lawful {{arrest}} which establishes the authority to search ... a full search of the person is ... also a 'reasonable' search."
back: arrest

front: How does the search of the person under Robinson (custodial arrest) differ from a Terry frisk in what must be shown?
back: A Terry frisk needs case-by-case justification that weapons/evidence are present; the Robinson search of the person needs none — the lawful custodial arrest alone is enough.

front: Riley v. California (2014): does the SITA bright-line rule reach the digital data on a cell phone seized incident to arrest?
back: No. Officers must generally get a warrant before searching the phone's digital data.

front: Riley v. California: state the Court's one-line answer for what police must do before searching a cell phone seized incident to arrest.
back: "Get a warrant."

front: Riley: why do the Chimel rationales not justify searching a phone's digital data incident to arrest?
back: Digital data cannot itself be used as a weapon to harm the officer or to effectuate the arrestee's escape (and the data is not destroyed by securing the phone).

front: Riley: what may officers still do with a phone incident to arrest, even without a warrant?
back: They may seize the phone and inspect the physical device (e.g., for a razor blade hidden in the case) — but may not browse its digital data without a warrant.

front: Common pitfall: an officer browses a phone's contents "incident to arrest." Why is this wrong?
back: Riley forbids searching phone data on the arrest alone; the officer must seize it and get a warrant. (Inspecting the physical handset for a weapon is still fine.)

front: New York v. Belton (1981): what is the SCOPE (the "where") of a vehicle search incident to arrest?
back: The passenger compartment of the automobile and any containers found within it.

front: Belton: within the passenger compartment, may officers examine the contents of containers found there?
back: Yes — Belton's scope expressly includes examining the contents of any containers found within the passenger compartment.

front: Arizona v. Gant (2009): state both triggers under which a vehicle search incident to arrest is lawful.
back: (1) The arrestee is unsecured and within reaching distance of the passenger compartment at the time of the search, OR (2) it is reasonable to believe evidence relevant to the crime of arrest might be found in the vehicle.

front: Gant prong 1: state the officer-safety/Chimel trigger for a vehicle search incident to arrest (verbatim).
back: Police may search "only when the arrestee is unsecured and within reaching distance of the passenger compartment at the time of the search."

front: Gant prong 2: state the evidentiary trigger for a vehicle search incident to arrest (verbatim).
back: When it is "reasonable to believe evidence relevant to the crime of arrest might be found in the vehicle."

front: Belton vs. Gant: which case fixes the scope/where of a vehicle SITA and which fixes the trigger/when?
back: Belton fixes the scope (the "where" — passenger compartment + containers); Gant fixes the trigger (the "when" — the two-prong gate). "Gant is the gate, Belton is the room."

front: What did Gant do to Belton doctrinally?
back: Gant re-tethered Belton to Chimel: Belton's scope no longer authorizes a vehicle search by itself — you must first satisfy one of Gant's two triggers to reach Belton's scope.

front: Common pitfall: treating Belton as authorizing an automatic vehicle search on arrest. Why is that wrong after Gant?
back: After Gant there is no per se vehicle search on arrest — you must satisfy one of Gant's two triggers first; cuffing and securing the arrestee usually kills prong 1.

front: Under Gant, what is usually the effect of routinely cuffing and securing the arrestee on the availability of a vehicle SITA?
back: It usually kills prong 1 (arrestee no longer unsecured/within reach), leaving only prong 2 (reason to believe evidence of the crime of arrest is in the vehicle).

front: United States v. Davis (4th Cir. 2021, persuasive): how far does Gant's first (reachability) prong extend?
back: To non-vehicular containers — officers may search such a container (e.g., a backpack) incident to arrest only when the arrestee is unsecured and within reaching distance of it at the time of the search.
cite: United States v. Howard Davis, 997 F.3d 191, 199 (4th Cir. 2021)

front: Davis (4th Cir., persuasive): why was the backpack search not a valid search incident to arrest on the facts?
back: Because Davis was secured and not within reaching distance of his backpack when the officer searched it, so there was no factual basis for a SITA under Gant's first holding.
cite: United States v. Howard Davis, 997 F.3d 191, 202 (4th Cir. 2021)

front: What is the precedential weight of United States v. Davis (4th Cir. 2021) on extending Gant prong 1 to containers, and how should it be framed?
back: Persuasive Fourth Circuit authority only — not a SCOTUS rule. Use it narrowly and pair it with the binding Chimel/Gant anchors.
cite: United States v. Howard Davis, 997 F.3d 191 (4th Cir. 2021)

front: On whether Gant prong 1 applies outside vehicles, what is the circuit posture noted on the SITA page?
back: Open but leaning uniform: the 3d, 9th, and 10th Circuits agree with the 4th and no circuit holds otherwise, but the 5th and 8th expressly declined to reach it — so it is not a clean split.

front: How does a booking/inventory search differ from a search incident to arrest in what justifies it?
back: SITA is justified by the arrest; an inventory is administrative caretaking justified by a standardized procedure (securing property, protecting against claims, guarding against dangerous items) — not by the arrest itself.

front: Common pitfall: rescuing a failed SITA with an inventory theory. What condition must the inventory still satisfy?
back: The predicate seizure/impoundment must itself be valid; a failed SITA cannot be saved by an inventory theory unless the impoundment was independently reasonable (the Braxton trap).

front: Illinois v. Lafayette (1983): what may police search as part of booking an arrested person?
back: Any container or article in the arrestee's possession, in accordance with established inventory procedures, as part of the routine procedure incident to incarceration.

front: South Dakota v. Opperman (1976): when is a vehicle inventory search reasonable?
back: When conducted pursuant to standard police procedures and not as a pretext concealing an investigatory motive.

front: Colorado v. Bertine (1987): when is police discretion during an inventory search permissible?
back: Only when exercised according to standardized criteria and on the basis of something other than suspicion of evidence of criminal activity.

front: Bertine: "Our decisions have always adhered to the requirement that inventories be conducted according to {{standardized criteria}}."
back: standardized criteria

front: Florida v. Wells (1990): state the central guardrail on inventory searches.
back: An inventory search must not be a ruse for a general rummaging to discover incriminating evidence; the policy must be designed to produce an inventory.

front: Wells: "an inventory search must not be a {{ruse}} for a general rummaging in order to discover incriminating evidence."
back: ruse

front: Synthesizing Lafayette/Opperman/Bertine and Wells: what two requirements make an inventory search lawful (apart from a valid impoundment)?
back: (a) It follows standardized criteria (Lafayette, Bertine, Opperman), and (b) it is not a ruse for investigation (Wells).

front: United States v. Evans (10th Cir. 1991, persuasive): why was the inventory of the carry-on bag upheld?
back: The officer followed the standardized inventory policy, there was no probable cause beforehand, and there was no evidence the search was a ruse — the textbook valid inventory.
cite: United States v. Evans, 937 F.2d 1534, 1539 (10th Cir. 1991)

front: United States v. Braxton (10th Cir. 2023, persuasive): why was the evidence suppressed despite the government's inevitable-discovery argument?
back: The impoundment itself was not shown to be reasonable — a companion had arrived at Braxton's request and repeatedly asked to take the backpack, a reasonable alternative to impoundment. No valid impoundment means no valid inventory.
cite: United States v. Braxton, 61 F.4th 830, 833 (10th Cir. 2023)

front: Braxton (10th Cir., persuasive): is compliance with a written inventory/property policy a safe harbor?
back: No — "the existence of and compliance with such a policy does not by itself establish a reasonable community-caretaking rationale." Policy compliance alone cannot save an unjustified impoundment.
cite: United States v. Braxton, 61 F.4th 830, 838 (10th Cir. 2023)

front: Evans vs. Braxton (both 10th Cir., persuasive): what is the contrast they teach about inventory searches?
back: Evans is the valid inventory (policy followed, no ruse, valid predicate); Braxton is the cautionary one — suppression where the predicate impoundment itself was unjustified and an alternative to impoundment existed.

front: Braxton (10th Cir., persuasive): what must officers consider before impounding property to support a later inventory?
back: Reasonable alternatives to impoundment — e.g., releasing the property to a present companion who asks to take it.
cite: United States v. Braxton, 61 F.4th 830, 833 (10th Cir. 2023)

front: Knowles v. Iowa (1998): does writing a traffic citation (instead of making a custodial arrest) authorize a full search incident to arrest?
back: No. There is no "search incident to citation" — without a custodial arrest, the search-incident-to-arrest authority does not apply.

front: Knowles v. Iowa: why did the Court decline to extend Robinson's bright-line full-search rule to a citation situation?
back: Because the concern for officer safety is not present to the same extent, and the concern for destruction or loss of evidence is not present at all once a citation is issued.

front: Knowles v. Iowa: once a driver is stopped for speeding and issued a citation, what does the Court say about the evidence-preservation rationale?
back: It is absent — "all the evidence necessary to prosecute that offense had been obtained" once the citation issues.

front: Virginia v. Moore (2008): an arrest violates a state arrest statute but is backed by probable cause — is the arrest and the search incident to it a Fourth Amendment violation?
back: No. A state-law arrest violation does not violate the Fourth Amendment; with probable cause, the arrest and the SITA are constitutionally reasonable and the SITA is valid.

front: Virginia v. Moore: what is the principle about the relationship between state arrest law and Fourth Amendment reasonableness?
back: State law does not define Fourth Amendment reasonableness — "it is not the province of the Fourth Amendment to enforce state law," and the Amendment does not require exclusion of evidence from a constitutionally permissible arrest.

front: Common pitfall: assuming a state-law arrest defect bars the search incident to arrest. Why is that wrong under Moore?
back: An arrest valid for 4A purposes (probable-cause-backed) supports the SITA even if it broke a state arrest statute — do not argue suppression on a state-law violation alone.

front: Thornton v. United States (2004): how far does Belton's vehicle search-incident-to-arrest rule reach as to who was in the car?
back: It reaches "recent occupants," not just those inside the vehicle — so long as the arrestee is the sort of recent occupant Thornton was, officers may search the vehicle incident to the arrest.

front: Thornton: "So long as an arrestee is the sort of '{{recent occupant}}' of a vehicle such as petitioner was here, officers may search that vehicle incident to the arrest."
back: recent occupant

front: Thornton v. United States: what did Justice Scalia's concurrence in the judgment propose that the Court later adopted as Gant's second prong?
back: Limiting Belton searches "to cases where it is reasonable to believe evidence relevant to the crime of arrest might be found in the vehicle."
cite: Thornton v. United States, 541 U.S. 615, 632 (2004) (Scalia, J., concurring in the judgment)

front: Trace the doctrinal arc of vehicle search incident to arrest from Belton through Thornton to Gant.
back: Belton (scope = passenger compartment + containers) -> Thornton (extends to "recent occupants" + Scalia's evidence-of-the-offense idea) -> Gant (the two-prong gate that controls when a vehicle SITA is lawful).

front: Birchfield v. North Dakota (2016): on a DUI arrest, may police take a warrantless breath test as a search incident to arrest? A warrantless blood test?
back: Breath yes — a warrantless breath test may be administered as SITA. Blood no — a warrantless blood test may not; it needs a warrant (or another exception).

front: Birchfield v. North Dakota: why does the Court permit a warrantless breath test but not a warrantless blood test incident to a DUI arrest?
back: Because breath tests are significantly less intrusive than blood tests and in most cases amply serve law enforcement interests.

front: Common pitfall: taking a warrantless blood draw as a search incident to arrest on a DUI. Why is that wrong under Birchfield?
back: The search-incident-to-arrest doctrine does not justify the warrantless taking of a blood sample — get a warrant (or rely on another exception such as exigency or consent).

front: Maryland v. King (2013): is a DNA cheek swab taken at booking from an arrestee held for a serious offense reasonable under the Fourth Amendment, and on what theory?
back: Yes — but as a legitimate police booking procedure (like fingerprinting and photographing), NOT as a search incident to arrest of the person.

front: Maryland v. King: complete the holding — taking and analyzing an arrestee's cheek-swab DNA is, "like fingerprinting and photographing," what kind of procedure?
back: "A legitimate police booking procedure that is reasonable under the Fourth Amendment."

front: Maryland v. King: in which doctrinal track does the DNA swab belong, and what is the key labeling caution?
back: It sits in the BOOKING track (beside Lafayette), upheld on a general booking-reasonableness rationale — do NOT cite it for the wingspan search of the person at the scene (it is not SITA-of-the-person).
